glossary.definition: A type of neural network where signals propagate in one direction from input to output without looping back.
glossary.sentence: the multilayer perceptron is a basic type of feed-forward neural network that, with one hidden layer, can represent any continuous function of the input signals and with two hidden layers - even discontinuous functions ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A neuron in the output or hidden layer that performs computation using weighted sum and activation function.
glossary.sentence: there is an output layer containing the computing neurons and providing the final output of the network.

glossary.definition: The process performed by neurons in the hidden layer to identify patterns or characteristics in input data.
glossary.sentence: at least one hidden layer that consists of computing neurons and usually performs the detection of features.

glossary.definition: The predefined groups into which output neurons classify input objects, corresponding to the number of output processing units.
glossary.sentence: the output layer is also pre-defined because the number of its processing units corresponds to the number of classification categories/classes.

glossary.definition: The knowledge base differs from a database. The database stores unrelated facts, for example, purchase transactions or grades of students. The knowledge base stores a representation not only of facts but also of relations among them.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge base differs from a database. The database stores unrelated facts, for example, purchase transactions or grades of students. The knowledge base stores a representation not only of facts but also of relations among them.

glossary.definition: needed to provide possibilities for an intelligent system to use knowledge represented in the form of a semantic network; based on principles: explore relations by following direction of arcs, check local relations, perform inheritance if needed
glossary.sentence: To provide possibilities for an intelligent system to use knowledge represented in the form of a semantic network, the inference engine is needed. The inference engine for semantic networks is based on the following principles: to acquire knowledge about some object/concept, explore relations of the object/concept to other objects/concept by following direction of arcs; to find knowledge about a specific object/concept: check local relations of the object/ concept; if knowledge is not found, perform inheritance (if it is possible).

glossary.definition: The inference engine depends on a knowledge representation schema that is used to describe knowledge.
glossary.sentence: The inference engine depends on a knowledge representation schema that is used to describe knowledge.

glossary.definition: In the first module of the study course, we considered two groups of artificial intelligence methods: weak methods, which do not use knowledge about a problem under consideration, but use general methods like search, logic, syntax-based computation, automatic reasoning, and strong methods, based on the great amount of knowledge about the problem under consideration.
glossary.sentence: In the first module of the study course, we considered two groups of artificial intelligence methods: weak methods, which do not use knowledge about a problem under consideration, but use general methods like search, logic, syntax-based computation, automatic reasoning, and strong methods, based on the great amount of knowledge about the problem under consideration.

glossary.definition: Incorporating knowledge into an intelligent computer system belongs to the so-called strong methods of artificial intelligence. Thus not all intelligent systems need the knowledge base because intelligent computer systems can also be developed using weak methods (symbolic manipulation or mathematical calculations). Machine learning applications and search-based planning systems are examples of such systems.
glossary.sentence: Incorporating knowledge into an intelligent computer system belongs to the so-called strong methods of artificial intelligence. Thus not all intelligent systems need the knowledge base because intelligent computer systems can also be developed using weak methods (symbolic manipulation or mathematical calculations). Machine learning applications and search-based planning systems are examples of such systems.

glossary.definition: Anyone can be considered a domain expert if he/she has deep knowledge (of both facts and rules) and substantial practical experience in a particular (usually limited) domain. Moreover, the domain expert has domain-specific heuristic knowledge that allows him to solve problems in a more effective way.
glossary.sentence: Anyone can be considered a domain expert if he/she has deep knowledge (of both facts and rules) and substantial practical experience in a particular (usually limited) domain ( Negnevitsky , 2011). Moreover, the domain expert has domain-specific heuristic knowledge that allows him to solve problems in a more effective way.

glossary.definition: The knowledge engineer is a specialist who knows well methods and tools of artificial intelligence, as well as who has knowledge how to describe and represent knowledge in the knowledge base. However, the knowledge engineer is not a specialist in all problem domains.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge engineer is a specialist who knows well methods and tools of artificial intelligence, as well as who has knowledge how to describe and represent knowledge in the knowledge base. However, the knowledge engineer is not a specialist in all problem domains.

glossary.definition: The knowledge acquisition system is an automated system that could be used either by the domain expert directly or by the knowledge engineer to describe knowledge and to build a knowledge base. Such systems can offer a variety of user interfaces, for example, they could offer creation of diagrams representing some scenarios from the problem domain or the creation of structured description of knowledge. Moreover, such systems include a set of tools used for the analysis and generation of knowledge and checking the consistency of a created representation.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge acquisition system is an automated system that could be used either by the domain expert directly or by the knowledge engineer to describe knowledge and to build a knowledge base. Such systems can offer a variety of user interfaces, for example, they could offer creation of diagrams representing some scenarios from the problem domain or the creation of structured description of knowledge. Moreover, such systems include a set of tools used for the analysis and generation of knowledge and checking the consistency of a created representation.

glossary.definition: Intelligent computer systems that emulate the human expert’s methodology and performance are called expert systems (Luger, 2009). An expert system is a knowledge-based intelligent system that solves problems in a narrowly specialised domain in a way and at a performance level similar to one of the human experts (Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).
glossary.sentence: Intelligent computer systems that emulate the human expert’s methodology and performance are called expert systems (Luger, 2009). An expert system is a knowledge-based intelligent system that solves problems in a narrowly specialised domain in a way and at a performance level similar to one of the human experts (Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: mean reaction time to the question “Can a canary bird fly?” is higher than the mean reaction time to the question “Can a canary bird sing?”
glossary.sentence: The previously described experiment showed that the mean reaction time to the question “Can a canary bird fly?” is higher than the mean reaction time to the question “Can a canary bird sing?”

glossary.definition: organize knowledge about birds in a hierarchical manner: general features characterizing all birds are associated with a concept “Bird” and, as a result, to find an answer people need to move along the hierarchical path in the structure of the memory.
glossary.sentence: This is because people do not store all features of a specific bird, but they organize knowledge about birds in a hierarchical manner: general features characterizing all birds are associated with a concept “Bird” and, as a result, to find an answer people need to move along the hierarchical path in the structure of the memory.

glossary.definition: stored at the level of a concept it belongs to
glossary.sentence: The researchers also have revealed that exceptional knowledge is stored at the level of a concept it belongs to.

glossary.definition: a knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge as a graph: labelled nodes in this graph correspond to domain objects/concepts but directed labelled arcs – to relations between them
glossary.sentence: A semantic network is a knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge as a graph: labelled nodes in this graph correspond to domain objects/concepts but directed labelled arcs – to relations between them

glossary.definition: represented by the title of “is a”, also “is a subclass of”, “is a subset of”, “is a type of” or “is a kind of”; allows showing hierarchical relations by relating narrower concepts to broader concepts
glossary.sentence: A relation “subclass-class” (also “subset-set”, “subtype-type”, “ subkind -kind”) is represented by a title of “is a” (also “is a subclass of”, “is a subset of”, “is a type of” or “is a kind of”). It allows showing hierarchical relations by relating narrower concepts to broader concepts

glossary.definition: includes a set of concepts/objects that share common properties; direction of the relation is from a narrower concept to a broader concept
glossary.sentence: A class/set/type/kind includes a set of concepts/objects that share common properties. The direction of the relation is from a narrower concept to a broader concept.

glossary.definition: represented by the title of “is an instance/example of”; relates one specific object in the problem domain to a class/set/type/kind it belongs to; direction of the relation is from an example to a class/set/type/kind
glossary.sentence: A relation “instance-class/set/type/kind” is represented by the title of “is an instance/example of”. It also allows creating hierarchical relations by relating one specific object in the problem domain to a class/set/type/kind it belongs to (Fig.7). The direction of the relation is from an example to a class/set/type/kind.

glossary.definition: represented by the title of “Part of”; relates parts of the object to the whole object; direction of the relation is from parts to whole objects
glossary.sentence: A relation “part-whole” is represented by the title of “Part of” (Fig.8). It allows relating parts of the object to the whole object. Without the defined parts, the object will lose some important properties or its original appearance. The direction of the relation is from parts to whole objects.

glossary.definition: represented by the title of “property/has a property of”; relates objects/concepts to their properties; direction of the relation is from the object to a property
glossary.sentence: A relation “object-property” is represented by the title of “property/has a property of”. It allows relating objects/concepts to their properties (Fig.9). The direction of the relation is from the object to a property

glossary.definition: a characteristic of an object/concept that can be measured by quantitative (numerical) or qualitative (descriptive) values
glossary.sentence: A property is a characteristic of an object/concept that can be measured by quantitative (numerical) or qualitative (descriptive) values.

glossary.definition: represented by the title of “value”; relates properties of objects/concepts to their values; can come only after the relation “property/has a property of”
glossary.sentence: A relation “property-value” is represented by the title of “value”. It allows relating properties of objects/concepts to their values (Fig.10). This relation can come only after the relation “property/has a property of”.

glossary.definition: allow specifying any other kind of relations (for example, like, own, travel, etc.) that cannot be represented by the previously mentioned types of relations
glossary.sentence: Linguistic relations (Fig.11). They allow specifying any other kind of relations (for example, like, own, travel, etc.) that cannot be represented by the previously mentioned types of relations.

glossary.definition: a process by which a narrower node automatically inherits knowledge from the network: a subclass/subset/subtype/ subkind node inherits knowledge from a class/set/type/kind node or an instance inherits knowledge from a subclass/subset/subtype/ subkind node or a class/set/type/kind node
glossary.sentence: Inheritance is a feature of semantic networks (Luger, 2009). Inheritance is a process by which a narrower node automatically inherits knowledge from the network, that is, a subclass/subset/subtype/ subkind node inherits knowledge from a class/set/type/kind node or an instance inherits knowledge from a subclass/subset/subtype/ subkind node or a class/set/type/kind node.

glossary.definition: also use a graph to represent knowledge
glossary.sentence: Structured knowledge representation schemas also use a graph to represent knowledge.
